meta.title: W166 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes W166 M‑Class engine — OM651, OM642, M276, M278, M157. Real UK costs, the pre‑GLE generation, and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes M‑Class W166 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W166 engine typically costs £2,200–£10,000 supply only, depending on engine — the volume OM642 diesel sits at £2,500–£4,500, while the M157 AMG V8 ranges up to £10,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500 depending on the variant. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W166 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642 V6 diesel (ML 350 BlueTEC) — proven across the Mercedes range and capable of 200,000+ miles with routine servicing. The M276 V6 petrol (ML 350 4MATIC) is also a strong performer, free of the OM651's EGR and turbo issues and more affordable to maintain than the V8 AMG variants.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes M‑Class W166?

acceptedAnswer.text: EGR cooler failure and turbo bearing collapse on the OM651 four‑cylinder diesel, oil cooler seal leaks on the OM642 V6 diesel, cooling system wear on the M276 V6 petrol, turbo cooling pipe failure on the M278 V8, and turbocharger and cooling system issues on the M157 AMG V8.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes M‑Class W166 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine and maintenance history. The OM642 diesel and M276 V6 petrol are the two most reliable options. The OM651 four‑cylinder diesel carries EGR and turbo risks — check history before buying. The AMG variants are reliable given their power output but demand meticulous maintenance and carry high repair costs.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes W166 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a high‑mileage OM651 (ML 250 BlueTEC) without documented EGR cooler and turbo history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A neglected M157 (ML 63 AMG) with turbo noise or cooling issues is the second. An OM642 diesel with visible oil leaks should also be approached with caution.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W166 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Loss of power, whining noise from the turbo, or coolant loss (OM651), oil smell or visible oil in the engine bay (OM642), coolant warning light or temperature fluctuations (M276), coolant loss or turbo issues (M278), or turbocharger noise or cooling system failure (M157) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes M‑Class W166 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £12,000 M‑Class, a £4,500 OM642 recon is 38% of the car's value — safe. Against a £20,000 ML 63, a £10,000 M157 recon is 50% — viable if you have the warranty, but a financial event regardless. Our 70% Mercedes threshold is rarely crossed on W166s with strong values, but running costs — especially on AMG variants — tell a different story.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes W166 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642 diesel and M276 V6 petrol can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing. The OM651's limiting factor is the EGR cooler and turbo — addressed proactively, it can still reach 150,000+ miles. The M278 V8 and M157 AMG V8 are capable of high mileage but cooling system maintenance is critical.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W166 M‑Class and the W166 GLE?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W166 M‑Class (this page, 2011–2015) and the W166 GLE (2015–2018) are the same underlying vehicle. In 2015, Mercedes‑Benz rebadged the M‑Class as the GLE to harmonise SUV nameplates with their passenger‑car counterparts (GLE = G‑Class + E‑Class). The facelift brought styling changes, new engines, and updated technology, but the platform and most engines remained the same. Costs vary slightly due to the facelift — see the GLE page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes W166 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate, in the vehicle documentation, and on the engine block itself. The 8th character of the VIN often indicates the engine variant. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes M‑Class W166 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the third‑generation M‑Class — from the OM651 four‑cylinder diesel to the OM642 V6 diesel, the M276 V6 petrol range, the M278 V8, and the M157 AMG V8. Real UK costs, honest failure data, and the generation that set new efficiency standards before becoming the GLE. Backed by 1,250+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes M‑Class W166 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W166 M‑Class SUV specifically — including the diesel range, petrol V6 and V8 options, and the AMG V8 that topped the range.

overview.intro: The W166 is the third‑generation M‑Class, launched in November 2011 as the successor to the W164. It introduced significant technical advancements including a new range of ultra‑modern engines, BlueTEC diesel technology with Euro 6 compliance, and a best‑in‑class drag coefficient of 0.32. Fuel consumption was reduced by between 24 and 28 percent compared to the previous generation. The W166 was produced until 2015, when it received a facelift and was renamed GLE to harmonise Mercedes‑Benz SUV nameplates with the E‑Class.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: W166 (Mercedes‑Benz mid‑size luxury SUV platform) • Predecessor: W164 (M‑Class, 2005–2011) • Successor: W166 facelift (GLE, 2015–2018) • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (the W166 was the only M‑Class body style until the GLE Coupé arrived later)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W166 M‑Class generated significant UK engine enquiry demand in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM642 diesel and M276 petrol variants as these SUVs enter their second decade of service.

items.quote: “The 3.0‑litre V6 turbodiesel is the W166's most dependable engine — producing 258 PS and 620 Nm of torque, it delivers effortless SUV performance with proven 200,000+ mile potential. Revised for this generation with improved efficiency.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want diesel economy and six‑figure mileage potential.

“The 3.5‑litre V6 petrol with BlueDIRECT direct injection delivers 306 PS and 370 Nm of torque — smooth, refined, and free of the V8's running costs. It set new efficiency standards for petrol SUVs.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want V6 refinement without AMG running costs.

“The 2.1‑litre four‑cylinder diesel has two well‑documented weaknesses — EGR cooler failure (cracking under thermal stress) and turbocharger bearing collapse, which can strike without warning and destroy the engine.” Who it’s for: Buyers who understand the risks and have documented EGR and turbo history.

“The 5.5‑litre biturbo V8 is a masterpiece — but punishing to repair. Turbocharger issues, cooling system failures, and the sheer complexity of the AMG engine bay add up fast. A full replacement runs well into five figures.” Who it’s for: AMG enthusiasts with deep pockets and full service history.

“The V6 diesel offers the best balance of performance, reliability, and running costs — more refined than the OM651, more economical than the petrol V6, and with a proven track record across the Mercedes range.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want the most sensible M‑Class engine choice.

“Often overshadowed by the AMG V8, the 4.7‑litre biturbo V8 delivers 408 PS of effortless power with better fuel economy than the 5.5‑litre. It’s the refined V8 choice without the AMG price premium.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want V8 power without AMG running costs.

rows.commonMajorFailure: EGR cooler failure / turbo bearing collapse
rows.ownershipVerdict: Risk vs Reward — budget for EGR and turbo work

economicsRule.title: The W166 rule
economicsRule.text: A £4,500 OM642 recon against a £12,000 M‑Class sits at 38% — safe. A £10,000 M157 recon against a £20,000 ML 63 is 50% — viable if you have the warranty, but a financial event regardless. Our 70% Mercedes threshold is rarely crossed on W166s with strong values, but running costs — especially on AMG variants — tell a different story. Value the service history first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W166 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM642 (ML 350 BlueTEC) — cheap oil cooler seal maintenance, proven V6 diesel.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W166 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M157 (ML 63 AMG) — turbocharger, cooling system, and V8 complexity add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W166 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM642 (ML 350 BlueTEC) — proven diesel refinement without the V8's running costs or the OM651's EGR anxiety.

problems.title: OM651 EGR Cooler Failure & Turbo Bearing Collapse (ML 250 BlueTEC)
problems.rootCause: The OM651 four‑cylinder diesel has two well‑documented weaknesses. The EGR cooler is exposed to constant thermal stress and often cracks under high load, causing coolant loss and potential engine damage. More catastrophically, the turbocharger bearing can collapse without warning — unlike minor oil leaks or sensor faults, this issue often strikes without clear warning and can destroy the engine.
problems.recommendation: If your ML 250 BlueTEC develops coolant loss, white smoke, or a whining noise from the turbo, get it inspected immediately — a £600–£1,800 EGR or turbo repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,200–£4,000 replacement that follows a catastrophic failure.

cta.label: Compare OM651 engine prices for your W166 ML 250 BlueTEC →

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leaks (ML 350 BlueTEC)
problems.rootCause: The OM642 V6 diesel's oil cooler seals degrade over time, causing oil leaks into the 'V' of the engine. This can lead to oil contamination of the engine bay, belt failure, and eventually engine damage if ignored.
problems.recommendation: If your ML 350 BlueTEC develops an oil smell or visible oil in the engine bay, get the oil cooler seals inspected immediately — a £600–£1,500 repair is far cheaper than the £2,500–£4,500 replacement that follows a neglected leak.

cta.label: Compare OM642 engine prices for your W166 ML 350 BlueTEC →

problems.title: M276 Cooling System Wear (ML 350 4MATIC)
problems.rootCause: The M276 V6 petrol generates significant heat, and the cooling system components — water pump, radiator, and hoses — degrade over time. The only common source of trouble in older and higher mileage W166 vehicles is the cooling system.
problems.recommendation: If your ML 350 petrol develops a coolant warning light or temperature fluctuations, get the cooling system inspected immediately — a £500–£1,500 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500–£5,000 replacement that follows an overheating event.

cta.label: Compare M276 engine prices for your W166 ML 350 4MATIC →

problems.title: M278 Turbo Cooling Pipe Failure (ML 500)
problems.rootCause: The M278 V8 biturbo has a known weakness — the turbo cooling pipes degrade and can fail, leading to coolant loss and potential turbocharger damage. Owners have reported needing to replace all four of the dreaded turbo cooling pipes.
problems.recommendation: If you own an ML 500, have the turbo cooling pipes inspected at every service — an £800–£2,000 preventative replacement is far cheaper than the £3,500–£7,000 repair that follows a failed pipe.

cta.label: Compare M278 engine prices for your W166 ML 500 →
